bayesian_inference.definition: A framework for statistical inference that treats parameters as random variables with prior distributions, updating beliefs to a posterior distribution using observed data via Bayes' theorem.
bayesian_inference.formula: p(θ|x) = p(x|θ) p(θ) / p(x)  ∝  p(x|θ) p(θ)
bayesian_inference.intuition: Bayesian inference formalizes learning: start with prior beliefs about a parameter, observe data, and update to posterior beliefs.
bayesian_inference.common_confusion: Bayesian credible intervals have the intuitive interpretation ('95% probability the parameter is here') that frequentist confidence intervals do not.
bayesian_inference.example_use: Given a Beta prior on p and Binomial data, the posterior is also Beta — a conjugate pair.

prior_probability.definition: A probability distribution over the parameter θ that encodes beliefs about θ before observing any data: p(θ).
prior_probability.intuition: The prior is your starting belief — it can be informative (based on past knowledge) or non-informative (expressing ignorance).
prior_probability.common_confusion: The choice of prior matters most when data is sparse. With large samples, the likelihood dominates and the posterior is insensitive to the prior.
prior_probability.example_use: A Beta(2, 2) prior on a probability p expresses a mild belief that p is near 0.5.

non_informative_prior.definition: A prior distribution that attempts to express minimal prior knowledge, allowing the data to dominate the posterior.
non_informative_prior.intuition: A non-informative prior tries to 'let the data speak' — it contributes as little information as possible beyond defining the parameter space.
non_informative_prior.common_confusion: There is no uniquely 'correct' non-informative prior. Jeffreys priors are invariant to reparameterization, but even these involve choices.
non_informative_prior.example_use: A Uniform(0,1) prior on p or a Jeffreys prior p(θ) ∝ √I(θ) are common non-informative choices.

posterior_distribution.definition: The conditional distribution of the parameter θ given the observed data x, combining prior beliefs and likelihood information.
posterior_distribution.intuition: The posterior is everything you know about the parameter after seeing the data — it replaces the prior as your updated belief.
posterior_distribution.common_confusion: The posterior is a full distribution, not just a point. Point summaries (mean, median, mode) lose uncertainty information.
posterior_distribution.example_use: With a Beta(α,β) prior and Binomial(n, p) likelihood with k successes: posterior is Beta(α+k, β+n-k).

posterior_probability.definition: A probability computed from the posterior distribution, such as P(θ ∈ A | x) for some set A.
posterior_probability.intuition: Posterior probabilities are direct, interpretable probability statements about the parameter — e.g., 'there is a 92% chance the drug reduces risk.'
posterior_probability.example_use: P(p > 0.5 | data) is a posterior probability that a treatment effect exceeds 50%.

posterior_mean.definition: The expected value of the parameter under the posterior distribution: E[θ|x] = ∫ θ p(θ|x) dθ.
posterior_mean.intuition: The posterior mean is the Bayes estimator under squared error loss — the optimal single-number summary of the posterior.
posterior_mean.common_confusion: The posterior mean is not the same as the MLE or the posterior mode (MAP). They coincide only under specific conditions.

posterior_median.definition: The value that divides the posterior distribution in half: P(θ ≤ median | x) = 0.5.
posterior_median.intuition: The posterior median is the Bayes estimator under absolute error loss — preferred when the posterior is skewed.
posterior_median.example_use: For skewed posteriors like the Gamma, the median may be preferred over the mean as a point estimate.

bayesian_point_estimation.definition: The derivation of a single-value estimate of a parameter from the posterior distribution, typically the posterior mean, median, or mode.
bayesian_point_estimation.intuition: Bayesian point estimation collapses the posterior to a single number — the choice of summary depends on the loss function.
bayesian_point_estimation.example_use: The posterior mean minimizes expected squared error loss; the posterior median minimizes expected absolute error loss.

bayesian_point_estimate.definition: The specific numerical value obtained by summarizing the posterior distribution with a point statistic such as the mean, median, or MAP.
bayesian_point_estimate.intuition: Just as a frequentist produces a point estimate from data, a Bayesian produces a point estimate from the posterior.
bayesian_point_estimate.example_use: The posterior mean (α+k)/(α+β+n) is a Bayesian point estimate of the Binomial probability p.

bayesian_interval_estimation.definition: The construction of a credible interval from the posterior distribution, providing a range that contains the parameter with specified posterior probability.
bayesian_interval_estimation.intuition: Bayesian intervals have the direct probabilistic interpretation that frequentist CIs lack — they are regions of high posterior probability.
bayesian_interval_estimation.example_use: A 95% Bayesian credible interval means P(θ ∈ [L, U] | data) = 0.95.

credible_interval.definition: An interval [L, U] such that the posterior probability of θ lying within it equals the specified level: P(L ≤ θ ≤ U | x) = 1-α.
credible_interval.intuition: A 95% credible interval directly says: given the data, we believe there is a 95% probability the parameter is in this range.
credible_interval.common_confusion: The credible interval and confidence interval are numerically similar in many problems but have fundamentally different interpretations.
credible_interval.example_use: A 95% credible interval for μ from a normal-normal conjugate model.

equal_tail_interval.definition: A credible interval formed by cutting equal probability from each tail of the posterior: [F⁻¹(α/2), F⁻¹(1-α/2)].
equal_tail_interval.intuition: The equal-tail interval splits the excluded probability evenly between both tails — the natural extension of two-sided frequentist CIs.
equal_tail_interval.common_confusion: The equal-tail interval is not the shortest possible interval. For skewed posteriors, the HPD interval is shorter.
equal_tail_interval.example_use: For a symmetric posterior like N(μ, σ²), the equal-tail and HPD intervals coincide.

highest_posterior_density_interval.definition: The shortest credible interval [L, U] that contains probability 1-α, defined by the property that the posterior density at L and U are equal.
highest_posterior_density_interval.formula: HPD: {θ : p(θ|x) ≥ c}  for the largest c such that P(θ ∈ HPD|x) ≥ 1-α
highest_posterior_density_interval.intuition: The HPD is the most concentrated region of the posterior with the required probability — it excludes the lowest-density tails.
highest_posterior_density_interval.common_confusion: For multimodal posteriors, the HPD may be a disjoint union of intervals.
highest_posterior_density_interval.example_use: For a skewed posterior, the HPD interval is shorter than the equal-tail interval.

equal_tail_credible_interval_method.definition: The procedure for constructing an equal-tail credible interval by taking quantiles from the posterior distribution.
equal_tail_credible_interval_method.intuition: In practice, equal-tail intervals are computed by taking the α/2 and 1-α/2 quantiles of posterior samples.
equal_tail_credible_interval_method.example_use: Sort 10,000 MCMC posterior samples; the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles give a 95% equal-tail credible interval.

highest_posterior_density_interval_metho.definition: The procedure for constructing an HPD interval from posterior samples, typically by finding the shortest interval containing the required fraction of samples.
highest_posterior_density_interval_metho.intuition: Slide a window of the required width across sorted posterior samples and find the position where the window is narrowest.
highest_posterior_density_interval_metho.example_use: The HDInterval package in R computes HPD intervals from MCMC samples.

hypothesis_testing.definition: A formal procedure for deciding between a null hypothesis H₀ and an alternative hypothesis H₁ based on observed data.
hypothesis_testing.intuition: Hypothesis testing formalizes the question 'is this effect real or just noise?' using a decision rule with controlled error rates.
hypothesis_testing.common_confusion: Failing to reject H₀ is not the same as accepting H₀. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
hypothesis_testing.example_use: Testing whether a new drug has a different mean effect than placebo using a two-sample t-test.

null_hypothesis.definition: The default hypothesis H₀ representing 'no effect,' 'no difference,' or a specific parameter value, which is rejected only if evidence is strong enough.
null_hypothesis.formula: H₀: θ = θ₀  (or  θ ≤ θ₀,  θ ≥ θ₀)
null_hypothesis.intuition: The null hypothesis is the position of skepticism — we assume nothing is happening until the data forces us to conclude otherwise.
null_hypothesis.common_confusion: The p-value is P(data this extreme | H₀ is true), not P(H₀ is true | data).
null_hypothesis.example_use: H₀: μ = 0 (the treatment has no effect).

alternative_hypothesis.definition: The hypothesis H₁ that is accepted when evidence against H₀ is sufficiently strong, representing the effect or difference of interest.
alternative_hypothesis.formula: H₁: θ ≠ θ₀  (two-sided)  or  θ > θ₀  /  θ < θ₀  (one-sided)
alternative_hypothesis.intuition: The alternative is what you believe is true if the null is wrong — the scientific claim you are trying to support.
alternative_hypothesis.common_confusion: The alternative hypothesis should be specified before seeing the data to avoid data dredging.

simple_hypothesis.definition: A hypothesis that specifies a single, fully determined distribution — the parameter is pinned to an exact value.
simple_hypothesis.intuition: A simple hypothesis leaves no free parameters — it uniquely specifies the data-generating distribution.
simple_hypothesis.example_use: H₀: p = 0.5 for a coin flip probability is a simple hypothesis.

composite_hypothesis.definition: A hypothesis that specifies a range of parameter values rather than a single point, leaving the distribution not fully determined.
composite_hypothesis.formula: H: θ ∈ Θ₀  (where Θ₀ contains more than one point)
composite_hypothesis.intuition: Most alternative hypotheses in practice are composite — H₁: μ > 0 includes infinitely many possible values of μ.
composite_hypothesis.example_use: H₁: μ > 0 is a composite hypothesis; it does not specify how much greater than 0 the mean is.

test_statistic.definition: A function of the sample data that measures how far the data is from what H₀ predicts, used to make the rejection decision.
test_statistic.intuition: The test statistic compresses the data into a single number that summarizes the evidence against H₀.
test_statistic.common_confusion: The test statistic must have a known distribution under H₀ to compute a valid p-value.
test_statistic.example_use: The t-statistic T = (X̄ - μ₀)/(S/√n) measures how many standard errors the sample mean is from the null value.

critical_value.definition: The threshold for the test statistic beyond which H₀ is rejected, determined by the significance level α and the null distribution.
critical_value.formula: Reject H₀ if |T| > t_{α/2, n-1}  (two-sided t-test)
critical_value.intuition: The critical value is the border of the rejection region — extreme test statistics beyond it indicate implausible data under H₀.
critical_value.common_confusion: The critical value depends on both the significance level and the null distribution, not on the data.
critical_value.example_use: For a one-sided z-test at α=0.05: critical value z* = 1.645.

decision_rule.definition: The rule specifying when to reject H₀ based on the test statistic, typically defined by the rejection region or a comparison to the critical value.
decision_rule.intuition: The decision rule converts the test statistic into a binary decision: reject or fail to reject.
decision_rule.example_use: Reject H₀ if the p-value < 0.05, or equivalently if |T| > 1.96 for a two-sided z-test.

acceptance_region.definition: The set of test statistic values for which H₀ is not rejected.
acceptance_region.intuition: The acceptance region is the 'normal' range of the test statistic under H₀ — data that lands here is consistent with the null.
acceptance_region.common_confusion: Falling in the acceptance region does not prove H₀ is true — it only means there is insufficient evidence against it.
acceptance_region.example_use: For a two-sided z-test at α=0.05: acceptance region is |z| ≤ 1.96.

rejection_region.definition: The set of test statistic values for which H₀ is rejected, also called the critical region.
rejection_region.intuition: The rejection region captures the most extreme test statistics — values that would be very unlikely if H₀ were true.
rejection_region.example_use: For a one-sided z-test at α=0.05: rejection region is z > 1.645.

p_value.definition: The probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one obtained, assuming H₀ is true.
p_value.intuition: A small p-value means the observed data would be very unlikely under H₀ — strong evidence against the null.
p_value.common_confusion: The p-value is NOT the probability H₀ is true, nor the probability the result occurred by chance. It is a conditional probability given H₀.
p_value.example_use: p = 0.03 means if H₀ were true, we would see a result this extreme or more in only 3% of experiments.

significance_level.definition: The pre-specified probability threshold α below which the p-value must fall to reject H₀, representing the acceptable Type I error rate.
significance_level.intuition: The significance level is the maximum tolerable rate of false rejections — choosing α=0.05 means accepting a 5% chance of incorrectly rejecting a true H₀.
significance_level.common_confusion: The significance level is set before the analysis, not after seeing the p-value. Choosing α after seeing data invalidates the test.
significance_level.example_use: At α=0.05, a p-value of 0.03 leads to rejection; p=0.07 does not.

type_i_error.definition: Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true — a false positive. Its probability is the significance level α.
type_i_error.intuition: Type I error is a false alarm — concluding there is an effect when there isn't one.
type_i_error.common_confusion: α controls Type I error, not Type II error. Decreasing α reduces false positives but increases false negatives.
type_i_error.example_use: Concluding a drug is effective when it is actually no better than placebo is a Type I error.

type_ii_error.definition: Failing to reject H₀ when H₁ is actually true — a false negative. Its probability is β; power = 1-β.
type_ii_error.formula: P(fail to reject H₀ | H₁ true) = β
type_ii_error.intuition: Type II error is a missed detection — failing to notice a real effect that exists.
type_ii_error.common_confusion: Type II error rate β depends on the true parameter value under H₁ and the sample size. It is not fixed like α.
type_ii_error.example_use: Failing to detect that a drug is effective when it truly works is a Type II error.

one_sided_test.definition: A hypothesis test with a directional alternative: H₁: θ > θ₀ or H₁: θ < θ₀, with the entire rejection region in one tail.
one_sided_test.formula: Reject if T > t_{α, n-1}  (upper one-sided)
one_sided_test.intuition: Use a one-sided test when you have a specific directional prediction and are only interested in effects in that direction.
one_sided_test.common_confusion: One-sided tests are more powerful than two-sided tests for detecting effects in the specified direction, but provide no protection against effects in the other direction.
one_sided_test.example_use: Testing H₁: μ_new > μ_old when you only care if the new drug is better, not worse.

two_sided_test.definition: A hypothesis test with a non-directional alternative: H₁: θ ≠ θ₀, with the rejection region split between both tails.
two_sided_test.intuition: Use a two-sided test when you want to detect effects in either direction — the default choice in most analyses.
two_sided_test.common_confusion: A two-sided test at level α has α/2 in each tail, making the critical values more extreme than a one-sided test.
two_sided_test.example_use: Testing H₁: μ ≠ 0 to detect any non-zero treatment effect.

hypothesis_testing_using_p_values.definition: The procedure of computing a p-value from the test statistic and comparing it to α to make the reject/fail-to-reject decision.
hypothesis_testing_using_p_values.intuition: P-value testing provides a continuous measure of evidence against H₀ — smaller p-values indicate stronger evidence.
hypothesis_testing_using_p_values.common_confusion: A p-value of 0.049 and 0.051 are not meaningfully different, despite falling on opposite sides of the α=0.05 threshold.
hypothesis_testing_using_p_values.example_use: Compute z-statistic, look up P(Z > z_obs) in normal table, compare to 0.05.

z_test.definition: A hypothesis test using a standard normal test statistic, applicable when σ is known or the sample size is large.
z_test.intuition: The z-test measures how many standard errors the sample mean is from the null value — extreme z-values indicate the data is inconsistent with H₀.
z_test.common_confusion: The z-test requires σ to be known. In practice σ is almost always unknown, making the t-test more appropriate for small samples.
z_test.example_use: Testing H₀: μ = 100 with known σ = 15 and n = 64: Z = (X̄ - 100)/(15/8).

two_sample_z_test.definition: A z-test comparing the means of two independent populations when both population standard deviations are known.
two_sample_z_test.intuition: Test whether two groups have the same mean when the population variances are known (rare in practice).
two_sample_z_test.example_use: Comparing mean scores of two large student cohorts when historical variance estimates are available.

one_sample_t_test.definition: A hypothesis test for the mean of a normal population with unknown variance, using the t-distribution.
one_sample_t_test.intuition: The most common test for a single mean — replace the unknown σ with S and account for the extra uncertainty via the t-distribution.
one_sample_t_test.common_confusion: The t-test assumes normality of the population. For large samples the CLT makes it robust to departures from normality.
one_sample_t_test.example_use: Testing whether the mean exam score equals 70: T = (X̄ - 70)/(S/√n) with n-1 df.

two_sample_t_test.definition: A hypothesis test comparing the means of two independent normal populations with unknown variances.
two_sample_t_test.formula: T = (X̄₁ - X̄₂) / SE(X̄₁ - X̄₂)  ~  t(df)  under H₀: μ₁=μ₂
two_sample_t_test.intuition: Test whether two groups have the same mean when variances are unknown — the most common two-group comparison.
two_sample_t_test.common_confusion: The pooled t-test assumes equal variances; Welch's t-test does not. Welch's is generally safer to use.
two_sample_t_test.example_use: Comparing mean blood pressure between a treatment group and a control group.

wald_test.definition: A hypothesis test based on the asymptotic normality of the MLE: W = (θ̂ - θ₀)² / Var(θ̂) ~ χ²(1) under H₀.
wald_test.formula: W = (θ̂ - θ₀)ᵀ [Var(θ̂)]⁻¹ (θ̂ - θ₀)  ~  χ²(p)  under H₀
wald_test.intuition: The Wald test asks: is the MLE far enough from the null value (relative to its uncertainty) to reject H₀?
wald_test.common_confusion: The Wald test is not invariant to reparameterization — testing H₀: θ=1 vs H₀: log θ=0 may give different p-values.
wald_test.example_use: The z-tests in logistic regression output are Wald tests for each coefficient.

likelihood_ratio_test.definition: A hypothesis test comparing the maximum likelihood under H₀ to the unrestricted maximum likelihood: LRT = -2 log Λ ~ χ²(df) under H₀.
parameters: df = difference in number of free parameters between H₁ and H₀ models
likelihood_ratio_test.intuition: LRT asks: how much better does the unrestricted model fit compared to the restricted model? Large improvement → reject H₀.
likelihood_ratio_test.common_confusion: The LRT is asymptotically equivalent to the Wald and score tests but often has better finite-sample properties.
likelihood_ratio_test.example_use: Testing whether a set of regression coefficients are jointly zero using LRT (equivalent to the F-test in linear regression).

chi_square_test_for_population_variance.definition: A hypothesis test for a normal population variance using the test statistic (n-1)S²/σ₀² ~ χ²(n-1) under H₀: σ²=σ₀².
chi_square_test_for_population_variance.intuition: Test whether the sample variance is consistent with a hypothesized population variance.
chi_square_test_for_population_variance.common_confusion: This test is very sensitive to non-normality of the population, more so than t-tests for the mean.
chi_square_test_for_population_variance.example_use: Testing whether the variance of a manufacturing process equals the target specification value.

bayesian_testing_concepts.definition: The framework for hypothesis testing within Bayesian inference, using posterior probabilities and Bayes factors rather than p-values.
bayesian_testing_concepts.intuition: Bayesian testing directly computes the probability that a hypothesis is true given the data, rather than the probability of data given the hypothesis.
bayesian_testing_concepts.example_use: Computing P(H₀|data) and P(H₁|data) using Bayes factors to compare models.

bayesian_p_value.definition: A Bayesian measure of evidence against a model, computed as the posterior probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than the observed data.
bayesian_p_value.intuition: The Bayesian p-value checks model fit by comparing observed test statistics to those from posterior predictive replications.
bayesian_p_value.common_confusion: The Bayesian p-value is not equivalent to the frequentist p-value and should not be compared to α=0.05 in the same way.
bayesian_p_value.example_use: Posterior predictive p-values are used in Bayesian model checking to assess whether the model fits the data.

degrees_of_freedom.definition: The number of independent pieces of information available to estimate a parameter, after accounting for constraints imposed by estimated quantities.
degrees_of_freedom.intuition: Degrees of freedom count how many values are 'free to vary' — each estimated parameter uses up one degree of freedom.
degrees_of_freedom.common_confusion: The sample variance uses n-1 df because one df is lost by estimating the mean. Forgetting this leads to incorrect inference.
degrees_of_freedom.example_use: The t-statistic for a sample mean of size n follows t(n-1), with n-1 degrees of freedom.

difference_of_population_means.definition: The parameter μ₁ - μ₂ representing the true difference between the means of two populations.
difference_of_population_means.intuition: The difference of means is the most natural measure of group comparison — zero means no difference, positive or negative indicates direction.
difference_of_population_means.example_use: Estimating the difference in mean recovery times between two treatment protocols.

duality_of_significance_tests_and_confid.definition: The correspondence between hypothesis tests and confidence intervals: H₀: θ = θ₀ is rejected at level α if and only if θ₀ is outside the 1-α confidence interval.
duality_of_significance_tests_and_confid.formula: Reject H₀: θ=θ₀ at level α  ⟺  θ₀ ∉ CI_{1-α}(θ)
duality_of_significance_tests_and_confid.intuition: CIs and tests are two sides of the same coin — a CI is the set of all null values that would not be rejected.
duality_of_significance_tests_and_confid.example_use: A 95% CI for μ that excludes 0 is equivalent to rejecting H₀: μ=0 at the 5% level.

likelihood_function.definition: The function L(θ; x) = f(x; θ) viewed as a function of θ for fixed observed data x, quantifying how well each parameter value explains the data.
likelihood_function.intuition: The likelihood function maps each parameter value to how probable the observed data would be — its peak locates the MLE.
likelihood_function.example_use: Plotting the likelihood as a function of μ for normal data shows a bell-shaped function peaked at X̄.

likelihood_inference.definition: A unified framework for statistical inference based on the likelihood function, encompassing MLEs, likelihood ratio tests, and profile likelihoods.
likelihood_inference.intuition: Likelihood inference says the likelihood function contains all relevant information about the parameter given the data and the model.
likelihood_inference.example_use: Profile likelihood confidence intervals for a parameter of interest, nuisance parameters profiled out.

numerical_maximization.definition: The use of iterative algorithms to find the maximum of the log-likelihood function when a closed-form solution is unavailable.
numerical_maximization.intuition: When calculus cannot solve the score equations analytically, numerical algorithms climb the log-likelihood surface step by step.
numerical_maximization.example_use: Gradient ascent, Newton-Raphson, and EM algorithm are all numerical maximization methods used in statistical computing.

sufficient_statistic.definition: A statistic T(X) that captures all information about parameter θ contained in the data: the distribution of X given T(X) does not depend on θ.
sufficient_statistic.intuition: A sufficient statistic is a lossless compression of the data for the purpose of inference — knowing T(X) is as good as knowing all of X.
sufficient_statistic.common_confusion: Sufficiency is relative to a model. T(X) = X̄ is sufficient for μ in a normal model but not in general.
sufficient_statistic.example_use: The sample total Σ Xᵢ is a sufficient statistic for p in a Binomial model.

two_sample_inference.definition: Statistical inference about the difference between two populations, using independent samples from each.
two_sample_inference.intuition: Two-sample inference asks whether two groups are different — the comparison is the fundamental question of many experiments.
two_sample_inference.example_use: Comparing mean test scores between students taught by two different methods.

confidence_interval_estimation.definition: The process of constructing a confidence interval for a parameter using sample data and knowledge of the estimator's sampling distribution.
confidence_interval_estimation.intuition: CI estimation is the practical procedure: collect data, compute the point estimate and its standard error, then form the interval.
confidence_interval_estimation.example_use: Estimating a population proportion with a 95% Wald CI: p̂ ± 1.96√(p̂(1-p̂)/n).

population_parameters.definition: Numerical characteristics of a population distribution — such as the mean, variance, or proportion — that statistical inference aims to estimate.
population_parameters.intuition: Population parameters are the unknowns that describe the true state of the world; sample statistics are our imperfect glimpses of them.
population_parameters.example_use: μ, σ², and p are population parameters estimated by X̄, S², and p̂ respectively.

summary_statistics.definition: Numerical summaries computed from sample data that describe key features of the data, such as location, spread, and shape.
summary_statistics.intuition: Summary statistics compress raw data into interpretable numbers — the mean, median, standard deviation, and quartiles are the most common.
summary_statistics.example_use: Reporting the mean, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum of a dataset as summary statistics.

statistical_estimation.definition: The branch of statistical inference concerned with using sample data to approximate unknown population parameters or functions thereof.
statistical_estimation.intuition: Statistical estimation is the science of making good guesses from incomplete information — trading off bias, variance, and computational cost.
statistical_estimation.example_use: Estimating the proportion of defective items in a production line from a quality control sample.

alternative_estimation_methods.definition: Parameter estimation approaches other than maximum likelihood, including method of moments, least squares, and Bayesian methods.
alternative_estimation_methods.intuition: MLE is not always the best or most practical estimation method — alternatives trade efficiency for simplicity, robustness, or computational convenience.
alternative_estimation_methods.example_use: Method of moments is simpler to compute than MLE for mixture models.

bootstrap_methods.definition: A family of resampling-based procedures for approximating sampling distributions and constructing confidence intervals without analytical formulas.
bootstrap_methods.intuition: Bootstrap methods use the data itself to simulate sampling variability — they are a computational alternative to analytical inference.
bootstrap_methods.example_use: Bootstrap the regression coefficient to get a CI that is robust to non-normal residuals.

large_sample_inference.definition: Inference methods that rely on asymptotic approximations (typically the CLT or MLE theory) that become valid as sample size grows.
large_sample_inference.intuition: Large-sample methods trade exact results for generality — they work for almost any distribution when n is large enough.
large_sample_inference.common_confusion: How large n needs to be depends on the problem. For heavy-tailed distributions, n may need to be very large for the CLT approximation to be adequate.
large_sample_inference.example_use: Wald confidence intervals for MLE estimates rely on large-sample normality of the MLE.

classical_test_procedures.definition: The standard frequentist hypothesis tests derived from normal distribution theory: z-tests, t-tests, F-tests, and chi-square tests.
classical_test_procedures.intuition: Classical tests are the workhorses of applied statistics — each is derived from the sampling distribution of a key statistic under H₀.
classical_test_procedures.example_use: The one-sample t-test, two-sample t-test, and F-test for ANOVA are the most commonly used classical test procedures.
